mnemonics: ACID Rain: A transaction's properties must be solid and reliable, like a good shelter, ensuring data stays intact (Atomic), correct (Consistent), isolated from others (Isolated), and permanent (Durable).
keyPoints: Atomicity: The transaction must execute completely or not at all; there is no partial execution.
Consistency: A transaction must transition the database from one valid state to another, preserving all database rules and constraints.
Isolation: Concurrent transactions must not interfere with each other; each must appear to execute in isolation.
Durability: Once a transaction is committed, its changes must persist permanently, even in the event of a system failure.
commonMistakes: Confusing Consistency (a transaction property) with data consistency (a state of the data).
Assuming Isolation means transactions execute serially; it actually allows for interleaved execution as long as the result is equivalent to a serial execution.
Thinking Durability only protects against power outages; it also protects against system crashes and other failures.
